stories.headline: Westminster Officer Fatally Shoots Armed Man During Domestic Violence Incident in Arvada
stories.summary:
A Westminster police officer shot and killed an armed man during a domestic violence incident at the Willow Green Townhomes, 6989 Sheridan Blvd., in Arvada's Lamar Heights neighborhood Saturday morning. Arvada police received calls at 7:11 a.m. about a disturbance involving an assault, multiple people including children, and guns inside the home. Officers safely removed two women and a child who were being held against their will before the confrontation escalated.

Witnesses reported two armed men remained inside the apartment. Officers observed one man crawl out a window onto the roof while armed, then return inside. When the man reappeared on the roof and reached for a gun at 8:03 a.m., a Westminster officer opened fire, and the man fell onto the roof.

Paramedics were unable to reach the man immediately due to uncertainty about conditions inside the apartment. A drone monitored the man, who showed no signs of life. The Jefferson County SWAT Team subsequently searched the apartment and found no one else inside. The man was pronounced dead at 11 a.m.; investigators found two guns near his body and a third inside the apartment.

The fate of a second man described by witnesses remains unclear, with investigators still determining whether he left before police arrived. The 17th Judicial District Critical Incident Response Team is investigating the officer-involved shooting, while Arvada police handle the underlying assault investigation.

stories.headline: Colorado Gov. Polis Says Canadian Strategy of Waiting for Midterms to Pressure Trump on Trade Unlikely to Work
stories.summary:
Colorado Gov. Jared Polis said Sunday that Canada's reported strategy of waiting until the November 2026 midterm elections to ramp up trade negotiations with the Trump administration is unlikely to yield significant results. Speaking on CTV's Question Period, Polis said Congress has limited ability to direct the terms of trade agreements like the Canada-Mexico-U.S. Agreement, known as CUSMA, because the executive branch holds broad authority over such negotiations.

'Sadly, having served in Congress 10 years, I have to tell you, that's not really the way our system works,' Polis told host Vassy Kapelos. He acknowledged that a Democratic-controlled Congress could theoretically apply rhetorical pressure or include restrictive language in appropriations bills, but said Trump is unlikely to respond to such pressure and that any meaningful legislative constraints would not take effect until fiscal year 2027-28 appropriations are considered in early-to-mid 2027.

Polis described the current U.S. negotiating strategy on North American trade as 'completely incoherent,' while urging both sides to better understand what the other wants from the relationship. The July 1 deadline for a review of the trilateral trade agreement is fast approaching.

The comments put Polis at odds with former Canadian Ambassador to the U.S. Frank McKenna, who said in the same broadcast that he 'totally supports' Canada's strategy of waiting out the Trump administration until after the midterms. Polis's remarks are notable given his national profile as a prominent Democratic governor and frequent commentator on federal policy.

stories.headline: Colorado Democrats Move to Counter Road-Spending Initiative, Legislature Wraps Busy Week
stories.summary:
Democratic lawmakers at the Colorado Capitol announced plans Friday to introduce legislation designed to counteract Initiative 175, a construction-industry-backed constitutional amendment that would require the state to spend approximately $2 billion per year on road transportation projects, including $540 million from the general fund. Democrats warn the measure, which has not yet qualified for the November ballot, would exacerbate the state's ongoing fiscal crisis following years of roughly $1 billion in annual budget cuts and a $1.5 billion deficit in the most recently passed spending plan.

'What we cannot do is fill a pothole and close a hospital,' said Rep. Andrew Boesenecker, a Fort Collins Democrat leading the legislative counter-effort. Lawmakers are seeking to prevent the initiative from reaching voters in its current form, arguing it would force impossible tradeoffs between infrastructure and essential services such as health care.

Separately, the legislature also advanced bills dealing with credit card swipe fees and state labor laws during lengthy floor sessions. Lawmakers additionally moved to adjust Proposition 123, the state's affordable housing fund, after finding that upward of 90 percent of enrolled local governments have failed to meet the housing growth targets voters approved in 2022. Legislators called the existing requirements 'inflexible' and 'outdated.'

Two bipartisan data privacy bills targeting warrantless government access to Coloradans' information were defeated during the week, with Senate Bill 70 — which would have limited government access to license plate reader data from providers like Flock Safety — voluntarily killed by its sponsors, and House Bill 1037 killed in committee, both amid strong opposition from law enforcement groups and, in one instance, Gov. Polis.

stories.headline: Home of Former Denver Mayor Wellington Webb and State Rep. Wilma Webb Nominated for Landmark Designation
stories.summary:
Historic Denver has submitted an owner-supported landmark designation application for the longtime home of former Denver Mayor Wellington E. Webb and former state Rep. Wilma J. Webb, located in the city's Whittier neighborhood. The nomination is scheduled for presentation to the Denver Landmark Preservation Commission on May 5 and to the Denver City Council on June 22. The property, built in 1903, is described as a quintessential Denver square with classical revival elements.

The Webbs purchased the home in 1971, and Historic Denver says it served not only as a residence but as a place where political decisions were made, alliances were formed, and a legacy of community leadership was established. Wilma Webb served six terms in the Colorado legislature and led the effort to establish a state holiday honoring Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., and helped create Denver's annual 'Marade' — a march and parade — that has become a civic tradition.

Wellington Webb made history in 1991 as Denver's first African American mayor, overseeing significant city growth including the near-completion of Denver International Airport, the opening of Coors Field, and downtown revitalization efforts. He also secured the Denver Nuggets and Colorado Avalanche to a 25-year commitment at a new arena.

'You cannot tell the story of Denver without Wilma and Wellington Webb,' said Denver Mayor Mike Johnston in support of the designation. The application was submitted in partnership with History Colorado and LaTonya Lacy.

stories.headline: Mild Weekend in Denver Gives Way to Rain, Snow and Cold Front Expected by Tuesday
stories.summary:
Denver metro residents can expect comfortable, warm conditions through Sunday, with afternoon temperatures reaching the 60s and 70s at lower elevations and low humidity. Light winds are forecast to keep fire danger minimal despite the dry air. The pleasant stretch is expected to be short-lived, however, as a changing weather pattern approaches early next week.

Clouds will increase Monday, with a chance of brief rain showers developing in the afternoon, though many may produce little precipitation and instead bring gusty winds. A cold front is expected to move through Monday evening, ushering in cooler air and more widespread precipitation.

Tuesday is forecast to be the most active day of the week, with steady rain developing across the plains before transitioning to snow by Tuesday evening around the Denver metro area and along the I-25 corridor. Light snow accumulations are possible at lower elevations, while mountains could see significantly higher totals.

The system is expected to strengthen from Tuesday night into Wednesday, with snow likely continuing and potentially impacting major travel corridors by Wednesday morning. Conditions are forecast to gradually improve later Wednesday as the storm departs, though cooler temperatures are expected to linger.

stories.headline: Evergreen Conducts First-Ever Wildfire Evacuation Drill as State Projects 1,000–2,000 Extra Fires This Year
stories.summary:
State officials this week estimated that Colorado could see between 1,000 and 2,000 more wildfires in 2026 than in a typical year, underscoring the severity of this season's fire outlook. The projection comes amid an ongoing statewide drought and a fire season that officials have described as particularly dangerous for both the Front Range and Western Colorado.

In response to the heightened risk, the mountain community of Evergreen conducted its first-ever wildfire evacuation drill, with residents and local officials practicing emergency procedures designed to streamline the process of getting people out of the area quickly and safely in the event of a fast-moving fire.

The drill represents a growing recognition among Front Range foothill communities that preparation and coordination are critical given the combination of dry conditions, dense vegetation, and the increasing frequency of extreme fire weather. Community-level exercises like the one in Evergreen are being encouraged by fire and emergency management officials across the state.

Colorado has already seen active fire conditions early in the season, and the state's fire preparedness agencies have been working with local governments to update evacuation plans and improve public communication systems ahead of the peak summer fire months.

stories.headline: Denver Weekend Events: Cinco de Mayo Festival, Derby Party, Supercross, Fashion Week and More
stories.summary:
Denver's first weekend of May is packed with events, headlined by the Cinco de Mayo Festival at Civic Center Park on Saturday and Sunday from 10 a.m. to 8 p.m. The free festival features Chihuahua races, a taco eating contest, a low rider car show, artisans, crafters, and family activities. The event draws tens of thousands of visitors to the heart of downtown Denver each year.

McGregor Square is hosting the 25th and final Denver Derby Party on Saturday starting at 1 p.m., marking the end of a 25-year tradition of Kentucky Derby viewing events in the city. Attendees can watch the race on a 66-by-20-foot LED screen across three levels of indoor-outdoor balconies.

The Monster Energy AMA Supercross Championship makes a stop at Empower Field at Mile High on Saturday afternoon, bringing professional motocross racing to Denver. Denver Fashion Week also kicks off its eight-day spring run Friday night at the Furniture Row Showroom on Bannock Street, featuring runway shows through May 9.

Additionally, First Friday Art Walks are scheduled across five Denver arts districts Friday evening, and First Friday Jazz returns to the Five Points neighborhood's Welton Street Corridor. The Denver Zoo Conservation Alliance is also celebrating Children's Day this weekend.

stories.headline: Avalanche Await Second-Round Playoff Opponent, Counting on Nicolas Roy's Continued Spark
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche have advanced to the second round of the NHL playoffs and are awaiting their next opponent, having earned the opportunity to rest and prepare while another first-round series concludes. The team is looking to build on momentum generated in part by forward Nicolas Roy, who delivered key contributions during the first round.

Roy's emergence as a playoff performer has been one of the notable storylines of Colorado's postseason run. His ability to provide energy, physicality, and timely offensive production has given the Avalanche depth that coaching staff and analysts say will be important in a second-round series against what is expected to be a formidable opponent.

The Avalanche's first-round victory sets up what could be a deep playoff run for a franchise that won the Stanley Cup in 2022 and has remained a consistent contender since. Denver hockey fans are closely following developments as the bracket takes shape.

The identity of Colorado's second-round opponent will be determined as remaining first-round series conclude. The Avalanche's preparation and health during the waiting period will be a key factor as the team looks to advance further into the postseason.

stories.headline: Broncos Active in NFL Offseason as Draft Results Draw AFC West Comparisons
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos are drawing attention across the NFL following an active offseason that included notable moves during the 2026 NFL Draft. The team's draft class and off-season acquisitions have prompted analysts and media outlets to compare Denver's roster construction to those of its AFC West rivals, with the Broncos landing in the top tier of multiple NFL power rankings.

The flurry of activity reflects the organization's continued efforts to build a contender around its core talent. Denver's front office has been focused on addressing depth and positional needs across multiple units, and early reaction to the draft class has been broadly positive within NFL circles.

AFC West competition remains fierce, with the Kansas City Chiefs, Las Vegas Raiders, and Los Angeles Chargers all making their own offseason moves. The division is widely regarded as one of the most competitive in the league, making Denver's draft strategy and roster decisions particularly consequential heading into training camp.

Fan and analyst interest in the Broncos' offseason moves remains high following years of roster rebuilding. The team's performance in the draft and free agency will continue to be evaluated as minicamps and organized team activities get underway in the coming weeks.

stories.headline: Denver Police Investigate Fatal I-70 Crash Involving Motorcyclists
stories.summary:
Denver police are investigating a fatal crash involving motorcyclists on Interstate 70 that occurred on a Thursday morning and resulted in the closure of the highway. Details about the number of vehicles involved and the identities of those killed or injured had not been fully released as of the weekend.

The crash caused significant disruption to I-70 traffic during the morning commute. The stretch of highway affected is one of the metro area's most heavily traveled corridors, and closures of any duration typically create widespread delays.

Fatal motorcycle crashes have been a persistent public safety concern along Colorado's highway system, particularly as warmer weather brings more riders onto the roads. Investigators were working to determine the cause and sequence of events leading to the collision.

Denver police have not yet announced whether any citations or charges are expected in connection with the crash. Further details are expected to be released as the investigation continues.

stories.headline: Colorado Legislature Recap: Nuclear Energy Bill Advances, Safe Haven and Puppy Mill Laws Signed, Marijuana Bills Pulled
stories.summary:
Colorado's legislature entered the final two weeks of its 2026 session with a flurry of activity across multiple policy areas. A bill to revive nuclear power development in Colorado advanced through the Capitol despite pledged opposition from environmental groups, reflecting growing bipartisan interest in carbon-free baseload energy sources. The measure is among the more consequential energy bills of the session.

Gov. Jared Polis signed two bills into law this week: a bipartisan safe haven measure extending the window for parents to surrender newborns from 72 hours to 30 days, and a package that includes a ban on puppy mills and expanded veterinary access. Both measures drew broad support across party lines.

Lawmakers separately pulled two marijuana-related bills — one addressing THC drink limits and another establishing potency-based taxation — without advancing them further this session. The decisions reflect ongoing difficulties in reaching consensus on cannabis regulation in a state that has led the nation in marijuana policy for more than a decade.

A bipartisan bill requiring the governor's legislative liaisons to register and publicly disclose their positions on legislation, similar to requirements for lobbyists, also advanced at the Capitol. Supporters argue the measure increases transparency in how the executive branch interacts with the legislature during the session.

stories.headline: Webb Home Landmark Push, Front Range Rail Progress Mark Big Week for Denver Civic Affairs
stories.summary:
Beyond the Webb landmark nomination, Denver and state transportation officials this past week celebrated a major milestone for the Front Range Passenger Rail project, which cleared pivotal votes from both the Regional Transportation District board and a state board, clearing a path toward a projected 2029 launch. The project would link communities along the Front Range with passenger rail service, a long-sought regional transportation goal.

Xcel Energy also appeared before the Denver City Council during the week to address concerns about public safety power shut-offs amid wildfire risk, telling council members that such shut-offs remain a 'last resort.' The utility has faced questions about its protocols as Colorado's wildfire season outlook grows increasingly dire.

Denver's climate office proposed changes to the Energize Denver building performance rules, specifically targeting restaurants and condominium buildings. The proposed adjustments reflect ongoing efforts to refine the city's landmark building emissions policy, which has faced pushback from some property owners and building types since its implementation.

The week also saw RTD under scrutiny after it was reported that the agency used the likeness of a Denver disability advocate on a bus wrap without her consent, raising questions about the agency's community engagement and communications practices.

weather.notable: Nearly 6 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I wish I could be a dog for like 20 minutes. Just to know what's going on in there. Then I'd be myself again. Because I don't think dogs have that great a time either.

topStory.headline: Trump Reviews Iran Peace Proposal but Signals Deep Skepticism, Citing 47 Years of Grievances
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump said Saturday he was reviewing a new diplomatic proposal from Iran to end the ongoing military standoff but expressed serious doubt that the offer would meet U.S. conditions. Speaking to reporters on the tarmac before boarding Air Force One in Florida, Trump said he had been briefed on the concept of the deal and was awaiting the exact wording. Shortly after departing, he posted on Truth Social that he could not imagine the proposal would be acceptable because Iran had 'not yet paid a big enough price for what they have done to Humanity, and the World, over the last 47 years.'

Iran's latest proposal would reopen the Strait of Hormuz to shipping and end the U.S. naval blockade, while deferring negotiations over Iran's nuclear program to a later stage. However, Ali Nikzad, deputy speaker of Iran's parliament, signaled Sunday that a guaranteed halt to Israeli strikes in Lebanon must precede any agreement on Hormuz, complicating the path to a deal. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said Tehran remained open to negotiations if the United States reduced what he described as excessive threats and provocative policies, while also stating that Iran's armed forces were prepared to respond to any aggression.

The standoff has been building for more than two months. Iran has blocked essentially all non-Iranian shipping from the Persian Gulf since at least mid-April, and the United States has enforced its own blockade of the Strait of Hormuz since April 13, deploying more than two carrier strike groups, over a dozen ships, and more than 100 aircraft. Last month, a U.S. Navy destroyer seized an Iranian-flagged cargo ship that attempted to breach the blockade. Trump was briefed Thursday by military officials on options in the Strait and on the ground inside Iran, and he has left open the possibility of resuming airstrikes, telling reporters Friday, 'Do we want to go and just blast the Hell out of them and finish them forever, or do we want to try and make a deal.'

The diplomatic uncertainty is drawing sharp criticism from Democratic leaders in Congress. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer called the conflict an 'illegal war' and accused the administration of bypassing congressional authority. California Gov. Gavin Newsom cited estimates placing the war's cost as high as $50 billion, and Sen. Chris Murphy of Connecticut argued that rising gas and grocery prices would undercut any administration claims of success. Trump pushed back against congressional pressure, calling it unconstitutional and arguing that legislative intervention was undermining U.S. negotiating leverage.

Israeli airstrikes on southern Lebanon continued in parallel, killing at least seven people Saturday despite a ceasefire agreement. The Israeli military issued evacuation warnings Sunday for residents of 11 towns and villages, citing Hezbollah ceasefire violations. More than 2,600 people have died in Lebanon since March 2, according to Lebanon's Ministry of Health. Trump said he would inform reporters of his decision on the Iranian proposal after reviewing its full text, leaving the situation unresolved as of Sunday.
